transcript: Final presentation of the day. Last but not least, hopefully. Um, I'm Grant, I'm the founder and CEO of Kinetic, and I'm here to present a new protocol that we've been working on. Um, uh, you guys have been kind of back-to-back highly technical talks, uh, so we'll keep this one a little bit more high level and, uh, for the more technically inclined, you can go to kinetic.xyz/whitepaper, uh, to access all of the gory, uh, details. Um, so, uh, who am I? I'm Grant Stenger. Um, I run Kinetic. Um, I've been, uh, I was initially crypto-pilled by Richard Craib in 2017, who runs a company called Numerai, and I've been building in the intersection of crypto and quant trading ever since at places like Polychain and Jane Street. Um, now I work on Kinetic. So, lightning tour, uh, you know, of the history of Kinetic, we'll blitz through it. We started building on Solana in 2021, initially building NFT trading infrastructure. From that NFT trading infrastructure, we started working on, uh, NFT aggregation in the Solana ecosystem. And then from there naturally, uh, we got very excited about aggregation broadly, so DEX aggregation has become, uh, one of our kind of core, uh, pillars of our business. Uh, we have a live DEX aggregator on kinetic.xyz that you're, uh, you can access anytime. We have public, uh, trading APIs. We recently announced last week that we, uh, did a big integration with Titan, and we have a number of big integrations coming down the pipeline right now. So if you work at one of these meta-aggregators or, uh, a wallet or a trading terminal or any of these, um, would love to, uh, get in contact with you and help, you know, hopefully we can, uh, uh, help get your users some of the, you know, tightest spreads on Solana. And if you run any of these underlying DEXes themselves, uh, we would also love to integrate you, whether it's one of the brand new exciting prop AMMs or one of the OG DEXes. Uh, we're constantly doing these integrations, so please reach out. Um, Kinetic is no longer just a DEX aggregator though. We have, uh, a full suite of products. You can imagine Kinetic is a, uh, kind of verticalized trading platform. Um, and, uh, you know, our mission is, uh, very well parallelized with, uh, the rest of the Solana ecosystem. We're really here to accelerate internet capital markets on Solana. And so, how do we do this? We, um, in this kind of vertical stack that we have, we built a fully featured platform. You can imagine the very top of the stack being, uh, our, um, community. The community is really sort of the beating heart of Kinetic and is what the rest of the stack is all, uh, all kind of building towards and is all here for. Um, we're about 10,000 strong across Discord, uh, X, and Telegram, and, you know, we're constantly running trading competitions and engaging with our, uh, community as much as we can. Uh, the next level of the stack serving our community is our UI. I would say, uh, we have, you know, one of the most cracked designers in the crypto ecosystem and an amazing kind of legendary frontend team shout out who's been doing an amazing job shipping, uh, a lot of product surface area for us. We have a mobile browser experience and a desktop browser experience, and these, um, are pretty well balanced for us. Um, on these, uh, uh, on our kind of UI, you'll see, uh, many different, uh, ways to interact with the Solana ecosystem. You might start with the Discover page where we have screening, filtering, searching, all the, uh, primary activities that you would want for sourcing new trades. We have a trench trading interface. We have all the analytics that you could want about pools and tokens. We have wallet tracking. We have portfolio management. We have ways to view other people's, uh, portfolios as well. We have a points program, leaderboard, contests. Uh, so we've built a lot over the past year, um, and, you know, we're continuing, uh, to build throughout. Uh, next in this stack, you can imagine, uh, once you've generated a trade that you're excited to do, you've discovered this exciting new token that you would like to buy, um, the next level is the aforementioned DEX aggregator, um, which we are spending, you know, a lot of time, uh, on. And from this DEX aggregator, this is where we've built, uh, a lot of expertise, uh, in the Solana ecosystem around, uh, the type of architecture that we would want to see in a level deeper. So this is, uh, preamble for, uh, what we're launching today. We're launching a new market structure on Solana, a DEX of our own. Um, and, and we're excited to kind of go to this, uh, deepest vertical, uh, that we've gone so far. So, um, blockchains are natively discrete time in the way that they execute trades versus continuous time in traditional finance. And for a long time, that's been seen as a blemish for the crypto ecosystem, one to be powdered over. You know, I'm very excited about the Shredstream's work and these types of details and their ways of sort of getting around this. But what if for a moment we considered what we used to consider as a blemish, uh, of the, uh, crypto ecosystem, this sort of discrete time nature of blockchains, if we considered it just for a moment to be a valuable asset? Um, and, uh, you might look to, uh, traditional financial literature. Uh, you'll see there's been, you know, much ink spilled around frequent batch auctions. Uh, what are frequent batch auctions? They are related to, uh, you know, they're similar to, you might say, uh, the opening and closing auction in equities where everyone submits an intent to trade, an order, and, uh, at the end of the auction, everyone nets out at a specific price. Um, and a frequent batch auction is just this done back to back to back to back over and over again. And, uh, one of the benefits of this is, uh, you know, the high-frequency trading ecosystem, you know, spends hundreds of millions of dollars beaming neutrinos through the center of the earth and, uh, you know, kind of working on executing on the order of, you know, microseconds and these sorts of things. But there's no real economically valuable information that happens faster than, call it, a hundred milliseconds or a second or something like that. Imagine if markets worked in such a way that once per second there was an auction, and it doesn't matter if you have the microsecond latency optimized execution infrastructure, uh, as long as you, you know, place your trade within the second, everyone will get the exact same fair price. Um, so naturally, uh, that begs the question, how might we want to bring that, uh, to the crypto ecosystem? And so, as I mentioned, blockchains are natively discrete time. Uh, the traditional financial ecosystem, it's been hard to innovate on these types of things. I think there's a lot of regulatory capture and these types of things, but Solana, I think, is the perfect place to introduce frequent batch auctions. Um, and so, uh, what, you know, what are we introducing here today? So, uh, we are introducing SUPA, what we call our SUPA protocol. SUPA is an acronym, S-U-P-A, Solana Uniform Price Auction. Um, what are the details here? So, in our SUPA protocol, uh, it begins, uh, very simply like a constant product market maker like you would see as one of the kind of primary, uh, you know, crypto-native, uh, market structures. Uh, but, uh, there are these frequent auctions that occur over and over again. And, uh, you can imagine in the same way that a, uh, pool creator might specify fee tiers, uh, you could specify different auction durations. Imagine a one-slot auction, 400 milliseconds on Solana, once per minute, once per day like the closing auctions in equities. Uh, you might end up with this sort of like hierarchical, uh, structure of these auctions and shelling points at, you know, right on the, you know, 4:00 PM Eastern and at the end of the hour and these types of things if you have multiple of these auctions. What are some other, uh, kind of upshots of a market structure like this? Well, uh, imagine, uh, Alice wants to place a million dollar buy of Solana and nefarious Bob wants to sandwich her. So he buys, uh, at a good price right before her buy and then sells right after her sell, capturing this sandwich profit. Well, imagine if everyone nets out within this auction at the same price. Uh, Bob is no longer economically incentivized, uh, to place this trade anymore. And so, um, you know, this, uh, auction structure prevents, uh, transaction-specific sandwiching, uh, in a very kind of positive way. So traders are benefited from this auction structure because of the sort of anti-sandwiching effects. There are also some, uh, details that we discuss in the whitepaper, uh, and, and, uh, you know, much more detail, uh, around some of the potential benefits, uh, to LPs as well. You know, roughly you can imagine running an auction, uh, right before the, or a, um, an auction right before the closing of the batch where some of the profits, uh, immediately go to the liquidity providers. This is what's potentially a, uh, LVR lever reducing mechanism for LPs, so they're losing less money, um, by, um, participating in this type of auction. Uh, users are not being sandwiched in this way, and so it's a kind of much tidier, uh, and nicer market structure, uh, for kind of all of the participants all around. So I think this is a very rich design space, uh, and one that we are, um, you know, pursuing, um, very, uh, deeply. And, uh, you know, we launched the whitepaper and, you know, when is the SUPA protocol going to, uh, go live? It's actually live right now. We just deployed it. You can go to kinetic.xyz to experiment with these pools. We'd love to get your feedback on some of the whitepaper details and, you know, potentially do integrations with some of the, you know, uh, big aggregators or whomever, uh, might have interesting flow. So I'm Grant Stenger. Hope you have a wonderful, uh, day of the conference and, uh, thanks for having me.

sources.title: How BAM extends Solana’s Lead: Making Internet Capital Markets A Reality | BAM
sources.snippet:
[Back to Blog](https://bam.dev/blog/)

November 12, 2025 • 8 minute read

# How BAM extends Solana’s Lead: Making Internet Capital Markets A Reality

Lucas Bruder

Jito Labs

Share

- _Solana's long-term success depends on deterministic execution infrastructure that scales transparently as the network grows. BAM exists to ensure that as Solana scales, the execution layer remains transparent, fair, and aligned with the applications and users who drive network growth._
- _BAM provides cryptographically verifiable transparency for sequencing that no other solution can provide: every ordering decision proven, not promised._
- _BAM will help everyone using or building on Solana succeed:_
 - _Applications gain the execution certainty needed to offer tighter spreads and predictable fills through Application Controlled Execution (ACE)._
 - _Traders benefit from superior execution quality that rivals or exceeds other venues._
 - _Validators get open-source infrastructure they can verify. When applications succeed on Solana, validators succeed._

# What Is BAM?

Solana stands alone: the only blockchain where trading SOL is cheaper and faster on-chain than on centralized exchanges. With BAM, that lead widens as apps gain the tools to outprice and out-execute other exchanges. BAM provides the execution infrastructure necessary to make on-chain trading not just competitive with centralized venues, but categorically superior.

The Block Assembly Marketplace (BAM), which Jito Labs revealed in July, represents the evolution toward a more transparent and application-driven transaction processing paradigm. BAM addresses a fundamental challenge that has constrained the network's growth: the inability for applications to provide predictable, high-quality execution guarantees to their users.

# Why Solana's Block Building Needed to Evolve

We’ve detected at least seven different transaction schedulers operating on Solana, each with distinct ordering logic and timing characteristics. This fragmentation creates fundamental uncertainty for applications and traders who depend on predictable execution. Traders cannot determine whether they're receiving fair execution or being systematically disadvantaged by opaque scheduling decisions made by validators with competing interests. The result is wider spreads and reduced liquidity as market makers price in execution uncertainty, while developers face inconsistent environments that undermine their ability to compete with centralized alternatives.

# Why Jito Labs Is Building BAM

Jito Labs helped pioneer trading infrastructure on Solana through our Block Engine. While these systems created significant value for validators and improved transaction execution, we've also watched how they've evolved. We've seen concentration dynamics emerge, observed the pressure toward private orderflow relationships, and recognized that without intervention, Solana risks following the same path toward opacity that degraded Ethereum's execution layer.

Ethereum's experience with proposer-builder separation (PBS) offers particularly instructive lessons. While PBS introduced competitive block building, it created perverse incentives: builders maximize MEV extraction to win block auctions, often at users' expense. This dynamic leaves the network stuck in a positive feedback loop that can be hard to unwind. Increased sandwich attacks and other forms of extractive MEV pushed orderflow private, as users sought protection through direct builder relationships. Builders offered rebates for exclusive access to this orderflow, and those with the most private flow could pay higher auction fees, winning more blocks and attracting even more exclusive deals. The result is a concentration of power among a small number of builders, increased opacity in transaction processing, and timing games that degraded network performance. Most concerningly, some dominant builders were operated by trading firms with direct financial incentives to exploit

November 3, 2025
[Expert Commentary](https://www.fireblocks.com/category/expert-commentary),[Industry Insights](https://www.fireblocks.com/category/industry-insights)
# The Case for Native Staking: What the Kiln Incident Reveals
[![ Headshot](https://www.fireblocks.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/Screenshot-2024-12-02-at-10.31.16 AM.png)
Salick Cogan
VP Onchain Products
](https://www.fireblocks.com/author/salick-cogan)
7 min. read
![](https://www.fireblocks.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/The-Case-for-Native-Staking_-What-the-Kiln-Incident-Reveals-_BH-1024x576.webp)
On September 8, 2025, a sophisticated attacker compromised a prominent staking provider’s infrastructure and walked away with customer funds. The breach at Kiln was not prevented by audits, penetration tests, or SOC 2 compliance, all of which were in place. The attacker used state-actor-level techniques that evaded every security measure.
The Kiln incident reveals a fundamental architectural problem: when institutions stake through external[dApps](https://fireblocks.com/glossary/decentralized-applications/), they’re often blind-signing transactions they can’t fully read and trusting infrastructure they don’t control. The incident perfectly illustrates why institutions need more than just custody solutions. They need integrated, intent-aware infrastructure that eliminates entire categories of risk.
## What Happened: Anatomy of the Attack
The breach began when an attacker compromised a Kiln infrastructure engineer’s GitHub access token, eventually injecting malicious code into the Kiln Connect API. The modification was surgical: when customers requested to unstake their Solana positions in significant amounts, the API returned the expected unstaking transaction plus a hidden instruction that transferred the withdrawal authority of the stake account to the attacker’s address.
On August 31st, one institutional customer connected to the Kiln dApp to unstake a position. They reviewed the transaction, their signing quorum approved it, and they broadcast it to the network. Everything appeared normal. But embedded in that transaction were multiple instructions that reassigned ownership of their staked assets. Days later, the attacker withdrew the funds.
Think of it like signing a contract that appears to be about one thing, but buried in the fine print is a clause stating “all your money now belongs to me.” The customer unknowingly signed away control of their assets because they were blind-signing a serialized transaction payload that was essentially unreadable. On Solana especially, even experienced blockchain engineers struggle to parse raw transaction data. The signing quorum did their job, but couldn’t see what they were actually signing. (Note: Kiln does provide an open source raw transaction decoder called Minitel, but it was not used in this case).
## Why It Happened: The Fundamental Problem with External dApps
The Kiln incident wasn’t just about a compromised backend. It exposed a systemic vulnerability in how institutions interact with external staking providers.
When you stake through an external dApp, here’s what actually happens:
* You initiate an action in a third-party application
* That application’s backend serializes a transaction
* Your custody solution receives raw bytes
* Your approvers attempt to ve

Introducing Drift v3: Built to Outperform  | Drift Updates

## Introducing Drift v3: Built to Outperform
December 4, 2025
![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6310e7dee49f08e5798eed50/693023e13e4b72ada17e0fe2_2025-12-03%2017.19.13.jpg)
[
Contents

Contents

[
](https://twitter.com/driftprotocol)[
](https://discord.com/invite/fMcZBH8ErM)
[
Trade Now
](https://app.drift.trade/)
![]()
![]()
Drift v3 is the protocol’s biggest performance upgrade yet. Rebuilt around trader execution, v3 delivers**10x faster fills, 10x improved liquidity, and a cleaner, more intuitive interface**than v2.
This upgrade was built for one thing: outperformance. Instant execution. Deeper liquidity. A suite of tools that helps you trade faster and with more confidence.
Drift v2 was the first to bring cross-margin and multi-asset collateral to DeFi on-chain trading. We built on Solana to deliver NASDAQ-level performance on-chain and with major Solana upgrades like Alpenglow, Firedancer and Jito’s Block Assembly Market (BAM), Drift is built to outperform as the network scales.
Since launch, Drift has surpassed 1.5B in TVL, $133B+ in total trading volume and $650M in open interest. Drift v3 is built to take those numbers and your trading even further.
**Drift v3 will be available on Dec 4, 2025.**New users will need an invite code to access Drift v3.
**DM**[**@DriftProtocol**](https://x.com/DriftProtocol)**to request access.**
## Why v3?
Drift v3 represents the protocol’s next stage of maturity: the point where years of infrastructure, liquidity, and execution improvements converge into a faster, clearer, more reliable trading experience.
**Over the past year, Drift has evolved in three critical areas:**
1. Execution speed
2. Liquidity depth
3. Trader experience## How Drift v3 outperforms
*The road to v3 was focused on improving liquidity, trading execution and speed. Here’s what that looks like in practice:*
### **Trading Execution and Speed**
* **10x faster order fills:**On Drift v3, 85% of market orders are filled within the same slot. A slot is Solana’s smallest time unit (about 400 milliseconds). On Drift, that means that trades are executed in under half a second. Instant execution.
* **15x faster take profit/stop loss triggers:**For BTC, it dropped from 15 slots (\~6 seconds) to 1 slot (\~0.4 seconds). This means your risk controls and exits now trigger immediately, reducing slippage and missed opportunities.
* **Gasless trading by default:**Unlike other DEXs, where you pay gas on every trade, Drift abstracts gas fees so that’s one less thing to worry about eating into profits
* **400ms oracle updates**: Prices update every 400 millisecond (roughly 2.5 times per second). This keeps quotes accurate and reduces the chance of stale pricing.
* **Scales with Solana:**As Solana increases block size and throughput, Drift automatically gets faster too, handling more trades per second.### **Liquidity**
* **10x reduction in slippage on market orders:**In Drift v2, large market orders could move the price by 20 bps. In v3, that’s been cut to just 2 bps. Traders now get far better pricing, especially on size.
* **5x tighter TP/SL execution:**Take-profit and stop-loss orders are far more precise. On major markets like SOL and BTC, fills are 4x tighter, while non-majors see up to 5x improvement. This means exits trigger closer to the price you set, reducing losses and protecting gains.
* **Introduction of Drift Liquidity Provider (DLP)**: Provides liquidity for both Perps and Spot markets. Community members can deposit funds into DLP to support market-making and earn a share of the pool's profits. Currently in the testing stage. Public launch in Q1 2026.
* **Expanded Market Maker Program:**With v3’s expanded market maker initiatives, long-tail altcoins now hav

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a hydraulic press being stopped by a crystalline shield as the Semantic Anchor. The press represents the predatory 'sandwich attack' squeezing a user's transaction, which is visualized as a vulnerable glowing orb. The crystalline shield that materializes to protect the orb represents the SUPA protocol's 'frequent batch auctions' and 'fair ordering'—a complex, strong, and perfectly structured defense against the attack. For the homepage text, I integrated '0.00%' diegetically onto a facet of the crystal shield. This high-impact number represents the core benefit of the protocol: eliminating the toxic MEV profit and slippage for the user.
visual_idea.description: A cinematic 3:4 portrait shot. Two massive, dark tungsten jaws of an industrial hydraulic press close in from the top and bottom, representing a sandwich attack. In the center, a single, small, glowing orb of data seems vulnerable. However, a complex, translucent shield made of interlocking, bismuth-like crystals has materialized around the orb, perfectly deflecting the immense pressure. The shield, representing the SUPA protocol, glows with cool blue light. On one of its largest facets, the number '0.00%' is subtly etched and glows faintly, as if it's a physical property of this protective material. The background is a dark, deep, and out-of-focus server room.

Two colossal, dark industrial hydraulic press jaws, meticulously rendered with a brushed tungsten texture, loom into view from the top and bottom, executing a powerful sandwich attack. In the heart of this crushing force, a solitary, small, luminous orb of data appears precariously vulnerable. Yet, surrounding it, a sophisticated, translucent shield crafted from intricate, interlocking bismuth-like crystals has flawlessly manifested, effortlessly diverting the colossal pressure. This protective shield, a physical embodiment of the SUPA protocol, radiates a cool blue light. Subtly etched into one of its grandest facets, the numerical value '0.00%' glows faintly, appearing as an intrinsic physical property of the crystalline defense. The background is a profoundly dark, deep, and intentionally out-of-focus server room, adding to the atmosphere of a critical, isolated event. The entire scene is bathed in high-contrast volumetric lighting, creating deep blacks, rich shadows, and cinematic contrast. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, photorealistic, Unreal Engine 5.

editorial.headline: Kinetic Launches SUPA Protocol to Eliminate Sandwich Attacks
editorial.standfirst: Grant Stenger unveils the Solana Uniform Price Auction (SUPA), leveraging frequent batch auctions to neutralize toxic MEV and improve execution quality. The protocol introduces institutional-grade fair ordering to solve the "continuous time" flaws of current AMMs.
key_takeaways: Grant Stenger announced SUPA (Solana Uniform Price Auction), a new protocol implementing Frequent Batch Auctions (FBA) on Solana Mainnet.
SUPA aggregates orders into discrete time batches (e.g., 400ms slots) that clear at a single uniform price, rendering sandwich attacks economically impossible.
The protocol is live at kinetic.xyz with a whitepaper available, targeting aggregators and institutional LPs seeking reduced Loss-Versus-Rebalancing (LVR).
editorial.article_body_markdown:
At Solana Breakpoint 2025, **Kinetic** CEO **Grant Stenger** challenged the foundational market structure of DeFi, arguing that the industry's attempt to mimic continuous-time finance on discrete-time blockchains is a design flaw that bleeds value. Stenger announced the immediate deployment of **SUPA** (Solana Uniform Price Auction), a protocol that implements Frequent Batch Auctions (FBA) directly on Solana Mainnet. By leaning into the blockchain's native discrete nature—where time moves in slots rather than milliseconds—SUPA aims to eliminate the sequencing games that enable toxic MEV.

 The core innovation of SUPA lies in its rejection of the First-In-First-Out (FIFO) model used by standard Constant Product Market Makers (CPMMs). Instead of processing transactions serially, SUPA aggregates orders into batches—ranging from a single 400ms slot to daily crosses akin to TradFi closing auctions. Within these batches, all orders clear at a single uniform price. This mechanism structurally eradicates sandwich attacks; an attacker cannot profit by front-running and back-running a victim if all three trades execute simultaneously at the exact same price.

For institutional investors and liquidity providers, the implications extend beyond simple trade protection. Stenger highlighted that SUPA is designed to address Loss-Versus-Rebalancing (LVR), a critical profitability metric for LPs. By running auctions that capture arbitrage value before a batch closes, the protocol redistributes value back to liquidity providers that would otherwise be lost to arbitrageurs. This aligns with the broader "Internet Capital Markets" narrative championed by entities like **Jito Labs**, moving Solana toward deterministic, fair execution environments that sophisticated capital demands.

Kinetic's launch of SUPA marks a strategic pivot from its origins as a DEX aggregator into a verticalized trading infrastructure provider. With a background at **Jane Street** and **Polychain**, Stenger is positioning the platform to compete not just on UI, but on deep market structure. The protocol is live now, with Kinetic actively soliciting integrations from major aggregators and wallets to route flow through these protected auction pools.
